sentences.text: Leon Wells.
Yes.
Yes.
And she helped me.
So I became a glazier.
And the funniest-- not funny.
It's tragic, tragic comedy.
She became afterwards a girlfriend, or whatever you want, to the deputy camp, Rokita.
And she became pregnant from him.
And she had her son afterwards in Munich.
He lived in Munich in '48, '47.
But afterwards, I don't know what happened because I did not follow.
I did not have any connection.
But you know, so the story goes, in tragic comedy, in a very terrible way.
But she helped me out.
And I became the glazier.
And it was only thing, the safest place in the whole camp.
Because even during the day when the SS men would used to sit at the window and shoot at prisoners as they walk by, I used to be able to walk every day, any place I wanted.
Because I took a piece of glass in front of me and was carrying glass.
They would not break glass.
They could shoot me.
They could kill me.
But they wouldn't break glass.
So-- and it was the only place in the camp where it was warm during the day in the wintertime because you cannot cut glass if you don't have warm hands.
So the other prisoners of war, if they did work, they used to run in for five minutes to warm up in my place.
So I had not-- I was hungry and so on.
But I did not have the problems of every day of hard work, and so on.
And nobody ever did anything in this-- in this small place, where in lumber you were outside.
And they could shoot you.
And they could torture you, anything they wanted, but not while you were working with glass.
Nobody touched you.
Nobody did anything.
Gebauer was the SS man, Gebauer.
Afterwards, where I told you I was a witness against him-- and his wife, Mrs.
Gebauer, gave out where he was hiding.
You know, she was also in this camp there.
But they had some kind of a problem, husband and wife.
And-- and afterwards, there was a guy, [INAUDIBLE].
But afterwards came Rokita.
He was a musician.
But he was a deputy.
And-- and all of these people I was afterwards as a witness at their trials.
And so in April of this year, the same '42, they started to expand the camp.
So we worked all day.
In the evening, we were building additional, carried wood and so on.
But it wasn't for me such a big problem because I was all the day in the easiest working conditions.
So it wasn't that I work all day and all evening.
So-- Tell me about the daily routine--
The daily routine was, in a simple way, that we were in this barrack laying people in very tight quarters.
And at 5 o'clock in the morning, we started to wash.
And at 6:00, 6:30 in the morning, we got our so-called breakfast-- you know, coffee and a piece of bread.
And afterwards, we might have had an appel, what we called, counting.
It was counting the prisoners, groups standing in one, two, three, four.
And some of them couldn't-- couldn't take it.
Some of them took it.
Because in the night, you came back to the barrack.
And you went to sleep.
In the mornings, you got up.
And some of them couldn't take it or had it harder during the day, being beaten and so on.
And so they run.
It's wires around the camp who was electrified.
So they run to the electrical wires to be killed.
You know, they got killed immediately, touched it and got killed.
Or-- it wasn't so hard to even to escape from the camp.
It was no big problem to escape.

The problem was to escape, was a bigger problem-- namely if you escaped, they killed 10 people from your group, and they went after your whole family, because everybody still had their family in town, including me.
I had all brothers and my parents.
Oh, yes.
Yes.
They bought it if somebody escaped.
And if somebody escaped in the night, they had appel, what they called.
And they counted.
And one is missing.
And where is the one.
Escaped?
They took out 10 people right from his group, shot him to death.
And by next morning, his family was brought over and being shot too.
So it wasn't the problem to think how to escape or when to escape.

That was an easy thing you could-- for example, for me, as a glazier, I could have escaped every day, because every day was that I went out even of camp to go to these private houses of the SS men that were supervised in our camp.
If a glass was broken, well, a window was broken.
And somehow very often a window was broken.
But they were 20 people or 15 SS men in 15 houses.
And one of 15 or something or a window or something wasn't in good shape.
So you had to-- to exchange it.
So it wasn't of any problem.
And once you went out, you could escape and finish.
But what good is it?
You know, and-- so the responsibility for other people in your family was bigger than the responsibility for yourself.
So it wasn't in any way to escape the problem.
And this is where they ask, well, why didn't you escape?
That wasn't the question at any time.
And so in 19-- in April 1942, they started very big expansion.
And they brought in Jews from Przemysl, from the south, from Stanislawow, from the border of the Polish-Hungarian or Polish-Romanian border.
And they started to bring in these people to the camp.
But we need expansion and building.
And at this time also, we expanded to 134 Janowska Street, from the original DAW.
[NON-ENGLISH].
DAW it was called before.
It was a military unit.
And we escaped through these.
And so we started to build.
And people from Przemysl, from Stanislawow, from-- and the Hungarian border started to be brought in because it too was starting to bring in people from small towns from around Lvov.
And we started to build.
But also at this time it started that people got sick on typhus.
That it was-- Typhus was running high.
And one day, I got typhus, end of May.
And-- and in the morning through the appel, I couldn't get up anymore.
I couldn't drink.
But it was the law of the group that if somebody is sick or dead, you brought them to appel, the other two near, that slept next to him.
And you lay them down.
So you counted so many people, and then one is here dead.
Or one is here sick, cannot get up.
And so I was one day that I was not being able.
And by the way, while I was going out every day to work, my brother from home used to bring me some food.
And now it's there waiting for me while I was getting out, going to the apartment to fix up the windows.
So-- and this morning my brother was waiting for me to give me a package of food.
But I was taken.
I was sick.
And the people, they're sick, the SS men came over, kicked you.
And you were taken over around the wires.
It was a wire-- a wiring ground.
And I was laying there.
And afterwards they came.
And we got somehow--
Sick people were brought.
One moment.
I will tell you why.
And from there they kicked us.
And we had to go to be shot behind the wires, to be shot there.
But they collected everybody in one group.
But somehow-- I don't know how, and so on-- you got enough strength.
And you get up.
And you walk.
As we came there, we were 180 people already sick behind the-- around the wires.
Did not-- they did not dig a grave for us, where to shoot us.
So they bought-- brought-- and we started to dig our own grave.
And we got undressed and only in underpants, in underwear, stood.
It was hot.
And I remember like today, my only dream was to get shot so I can drink my own blood because I was so thirsty.
And you stand there, stand there.
And they called my name.
And they had the list, who would be shot today.
And they called my name.
So before I knew, I started to walk down as the next two guys, because they called everybody when they had it ready, the grave.
Nothing.
Nothing.
Nobody thought about anything.
You know, if you are in a June day, at 90, 95 degrees, and you are thirsty, you're standing, sweating with high temperature, you don't analyze.
You don't start to scream.
You don't do anything.
As I said, my only dream was that the shooting would help me.
You will get the blood to drink.
What could be better?
That is your only solution.
You will be saved.
That's your savior.
And I started to walk down.
And they said, no.
Come with me.
So I walked, you don't think.
And they say, come here.
This is it.
There were so many SS men.
And a few askaris-- the askaris, the Russian prisoners of war that were looking that we don't run away.
They were only there to watch us if we don't run away, that nobody runs away, where the SS men did the killing.
Two people, eight down, across in two directions.
They laid properly.
They were shot.
Next two people.
They put down a little ground on top of them.
Next two people went down, laid down.
They were shot in the back and finished.
So as I say, your only dream is to get something to drink, nothing else.
You have high temperature.
You are in the sun standing.
What else can you think about it, and so on.
And so you lay down.
And so I start to walk down.
And they said, no.
Come with me.
So I walked with him.
You don't question, or you don't think.
And the whole idea was that it started lunchtime in the camp.
And there they shot a man.
And by law, by German law, by SS law, once you are sentenced to death also by any law, you don't bring him back.
But they did not want to bring the dead men to our grave.
So they said, he's going to the-- why can't he carry him?
You know.
So it was their fault that they took me, not anybody else.
They were-- so in any case, I went there to bring these dead men.
I went to the-- near the kitchen.
The line in the kitchen, there was a dead man.
And I took him at his feet and pulled him behind me.
I couldn't carry him or anything-- pulled.
And these SS men walked a few steps, couldn't crawl after me.
How quickly could I with so many degrees of temperature and hot and weak-- and pulled him slowly.
And he walked a few feet, stopped, waited for me, and I pulled him.
And when I reached him, he walked again a few feet.
And-- so be-- And it was, as I say, to lunchtime.
So everybody started to come into the kitchen.
They were all brigades brought back to the camp.
And so everybody came into-- and I saw all these people.
Without even thinking, somehow I got strength again.
And I took this dead body and pushed it against these SS men.
And I walked in, into this group of people, thousands, hundreds of people.
There were 2,000 people in camp.
So there were maybe 600, 700 people for lunch coming.
These SS men stupid-- stupidly called out my name.
Weliczker, Weliczker.
But when I heard, he calls my name here, I walked over here.
I couldn't run.
I couldn't do anything.
I only went by the sound.
If he was here, I heard the sound coming from here, I went here.
That was all.
And so he was also scared that he took me because he had a higher group too.
And after a few times calling-- I don't know-- five, six, eight times calling me-- and I was sitting.
Where every time he called me, I sit.
I stayed there til after lunch.
And after lunch, I went with my brigade back to the group where I was a glazier.
And I went into this glazier shop.
And there I laid down under the table and told all my friends that were together with me that if in the night-- in appel they say-- 80 people and one dead, I'm dead.
But if they say 79 people and 1 escaped, just say he did not escape.
He's laying here.
Let them come and take me.
I'm laying, I'm not escaping at all.
So I laid.
I probably fell right away asleep.
I don't know.
Next morning they came back.
And I said, so what happened last night?
They said one is dead.
So if one is dead, I'm dead.
I'm not anymore alive.
So I walked out of the camp.
Because I took a piece of glass and walked out.
And I walked till home, which was quite a distance.
But somehow, slowly walking, walking.
And what did you find when you got home?
My mother, everything-- everybody was still.
And they got what happened.
I told them quickly.
They put me down.
And by-- and I went right into bed.
And they called up the hospital.
I took my brother's name, Aaron.
He was at home too.
[INAUDIBLE] has typhus.
So they took me to the hospital.
In Poland, the hospitals were run by nuns, always sisters.
You know, it was-- this was the sister's job, hospitals to be sisters.
They did not even look who I am or why I am there, did not ask any question.
You  know, they did not report that there is a Jew or anything.
Thinking today back, you think a little bit differently because they could have been in trouble.
But they did not.
I was-- I never-- they could have seen right away that I'm Jewish, I'm running away from something.
Or, not running away-- my brother and family-- report these Jews.
Did not report me and anything.
And I was for two weeks in the hospital.
I had pneumonia, both sides pneumonia, and typhus.
I don't know what happened.
I got out.
And once I got out, my parents felt the best thing is to send me to Stojanow with my friend's parents.
And there I will find another job.
And that will be the solution.
Because what else could they do with me?
So I got out and within two days, my young brother, my child Jacob, walked with me.
He walked with me the 60 miles to Stojanow.
He took me to Stojanow.
And this is how I came back to Stojanow, got a job on a farm.
Within two or three days, farmers always needed somebody to take care of the animals.
And they were concerned about hiring a Jew?
No.
No.
Somehow-- it's free labor.
They fed me.
And I slept in the stables.
And nobody even-- I don't know.
I don't remember.
You see, a lot of people-- and this is what I say.
You can today analyze it and say what in a way and how.
But at this time, you somehow did not think.
You lived in a different world.
Your mind stopped to work.
You don't think.
You do.
Like any animal that gets fat or something, you feed-- what we understand about animals.
I don't know how they think.
But you don't think.
You don't analyze.
You don't say if I do this, if I do this.
It is like in a different world.
You don't live in your world.
And so I worked in a farm, never went to my grandparents or anybody to visit.
There were my four sisters with my grandparents.
Sent there before.
And my oldest sister worked in a farm and took care of the other three little girls.
They all worked on a farm because in a farm, even if you are 80 years old, you can do certain work.
So they work. every-- I went there.
I saw them maybe for 10, 15 minutes.
I don't even know what, if I talked to them or not.
I left.
And it was the last time I saw them.
I left to the farm, working on the farm until on the farm I somehow dreamt that my mother and all-- and before Yom Kippur I came back and-- and asked what's happening with my mother, my sisters.
Because somehow I heard there was an aktion in this town.
I knew it was an aktion in this town.
In Stojanow.
Yes, before Yom Kippur, in nineteen forty--
Two.
And-- and they were all taken.
And my mother in Lemberg was also taken.
They told me that three days before they got a letter from Lwow that my mother was taken in the aktion and my father was.
And only my two brothers are still in Lwow.
Yeah.
Stojanow.
And the farm-- and nobody really knows how.
Somehow under different circumstances, they-- but you know, it is hearsay and so on, but I don't want-- because I don't know really what happened.
They were taken to Belzec.
And I-- afterwards was a liquidation of Stojanow after this aktion.
And they all moved to [?
Rzeszow ?], the ones that were left, the few people that were left, moved to [?
Rzeszow ?].
And among them were one of my uncles-- two of my uncles, and my grandfather.
My grandmother and all of these were taken in the aktion.
And I went there to [?
Rzeszow ?] too.
In Lwow, with my brothers.
And after a while in [?
Rzeszow ?], I went back to Lwow to be with my two brothers and my father.
My mental state was of-- you see, a lot of people don't understand till today.
You don't want to survive.
You don't want to live anymore.
It is not, why didn't you fight, and so on.
Who cared?
You know, we-- none of us wants to tell it.
But I will tell you something.
When I was afterwards in concentration camp, coming back and so on, if you talked to people, the whole idea was, let Germany win.
They're begging that Germany should win the war.
To show the world what it means.
You have such a bitterness against the whole world, that this is the only-- it's not reversed that you love the whole world, let them survive in-- you don't care about anybody.
You're already finished.
You want the absolute end.
